# INTRODUCTION

## Context and Motivation

Roughly one in every six units of value added produced in Spain goes undeclared to the tax authorities. According to @medinaShadowEconomiesWorld2018, the share of Spain's underground economy has systematically ranged between 17% and 22% of GDP, a figure that has remained stable over the last three decades and is substantially higher than in northern European economies (7-10%), though comparable to other Mediterranean economies such as Italy (20-24%). An underground economy of this magnitude implies a loss of potential tax revenue, distorts competition between formal and informal (or underground) firms, and complicates the design of economic policy.

The causes of informality are not well understood. The literature puts forward three explanations. The first is fiscal: when the tax burden is high, agents shift a fraction of their activity to the underground sector to reduce their tax load [@OrsiInformal; @herranzTaxEvasionFiscal2023]. The second is efficiency-based: if the informal sector has higher productivity than the formal one, agents will gain more by carrying out their activity in the underground sector. The third is institutional: the probability and severity of penalties for evasion would determine how much activity stays hidden, independent of tax rates [@ulysseaFirmsInformalityDevelopment2018]. Disentangling which of these three explanations dominates tells us whether policy can actually reduce informality: if the mechanism is fiscal, a tax reform can reduce it; if it is efficiency-based, fiscal policy has a limited role; if it is about enforcement, the solution lies with the Tax Administration.

This work estimates a Dynamic Stochastic General Equilibrium (DSGE) model with an underground economy for the Spanish economy and quantitatively identifies its transmission mechanisms.

## The Estimation Challenge

DSGE models formalize the economy as the outcome of rational agents' decisions under uncertainty, and since @kydlandTimeBuildAggregate1982 they have become the standard tool for connecting macroeconomic theory with aggregate data.

Estimating these models entails a high computational cost: for every new proposed set of parameters, the full model must be solved and the Kalman filter applied to evaluate its likelihood — a demanding procedure that must be repeated continuously within the algorithm's loop. On top of this comes an identification problem that @CanovaSala2009 formally document: very diverse combinations of structural parameters generate practically indistinguishable aggregate dynamics, and the likelihood function presents flat regions where numerical optimization fails.

Bayesian inference has become the standard approach for handling these challenges [@SmetsWouters2007]. Priors act as a numerical regularizer over the flat regions of the likelihood and as an anchor that prevents the estimation from converging on economically meaningless values.

## The Literature on DSGE Models with an Underground Economy

Applying DSGE models to the study of the underground economy is relatively recent. The pioneering work is @OrsiInformal, who build a two-sector RBC model — formal and informal — in which firms decide each quarter what fraction of their output to declare to the tax authorities. Households choose how to allocate hours of work between the two sectors based on the after-tax profitability differential. The government imposes an explicit fiscal system (corporate tax, personal income tax, social security contributions) and carries out inspections with an endogenous probability. The estimation on Italian data for 1982–2006 places the underground share between 20% and 24% of GDP and concludes that fiscal shocks, not productivity shocks, explain most of the fluctuations in informality.

@herranzTaxEvasionFiscal2023 apply a similar framework to the Spanish economy for the period 1985–2015 and find that the underground economy has, on average, contributed roughly 23% to the accumulation of public debt. Their model focuses on the interaction between fiscal policy, evasion, and debt, whereas the present work emphasizes the transmission mechanisms of fiscal shocks and the comparison of alternative policies through steady-state counterfactuals.

@ulysseaFirmsInformalityDevelopment2018 distinguishes between the extensive margin (firms operating entirely outside the formal sector) and the intensive margin (registered firms that hide a fraction of their output), and shows that the two margins respond differently to policy reforms. The @OrsiInformal model estimated here operates on the intensive margin, assuming that all firms maintain positive formal activity.

## Contribution and Structure of This Work

This work is structured around two methodological axes. The first is full Bayesian estimation via the *No-U-Turn Sampler* (*NUTS*), a Hamiltonian-dynamics-based Markov Chain Monte Carlo (*MCMC*) algorithm substantially more efficient than traditional Metropolis-Hastings samplers, applied to a DSGE model with eight structural shocks. The implementation is carried out entirely in Julia, exploiting the language's composability to embed the model solver directly inside the MCMC loop without external calls. In addition, an alternative methodological route based on Deep Learning and Indirect Inference is documented, explored with the aim of eliminating the dimensionality problem for more complex versions of the model.


The second contribution is applied: the @OrsiInformal model is estimated on Spanish data for 1996–2019. The sample stops at the fourth quarter of 2019 to keep the estimation of the structural parameters free from the distortion the pandemic shock would introduce into investment and capital accumulation patterns. With five quarterly observable time series, this yields the first estimation of this model for the Spanish economy. Comparing these estimates with the Italian case and with the evidence of @herranzTaxEvasionFiscal2023 identifies a distinctive feature of the Spanish economy: the great ease with which agents move between the formal and underground sectors. This translates into a substitution elasticity that is roughly double the Italian one (~2.0 versus ~1.0), meaning that households in Spain are much more prone to shift their work toward the underground economy when conditions in the formal market worsen.

The main results confirm the fiscal hypothesis: shocks to corporate tax and social security contributions explain most of the fluctuations in the underground share, while technology shocks play a secondary role. The model allows four counterfactual policy scenarios to be quantified, leading to the conclusion that a corporate tax cut is almost self-financing in the long run, that inspection is a minor complement to fiscal reform rather than a substitute for it, and that the composition of the tax burden, not just its level, determines macroeconomic outcomes.

Chapters @sec-rbc, @sec-metodologies, and @sec-orsi-turino present the theoretical and methodological foundations. Chapters @sec-simulat and @sec-real cover the validation on simulated data and the empirical estimation for Spain. @sec-analisis develops the policy analysis and counterfactuals, and @sec-conclusions gathers the conclusions and limitations.
